title: Social Media Apps Found to Be Addictive in Court
summary: A court in Los Angeles has made a big decision about social media apps like Instagram and YouTube. The jury said these apps are addictive and were made that way on purpose. The companies that own them, Meta and Google, have to pay $6 million to a young woman named Kaley because she got sick from using the apps. She felt sad and had bad thoughts about her body. The companies say they will appeal, which means they want to change the decision. Experts think this ruling is very important and could change how social media works in the future. Other apps like TikTok and Snapchat settled their cases before going to court. This shows that tech companies are worried about these lawsuits.

background_read: This article is about a court case in Los Angeles where a jury decided that social media apps like Instagram and YouTube are addictive. The companies that own these apps, Meta and Google, were ordered to pay money to a young woman named Kaley because she got sick from using them. The companies say they will appeal, which means they want a higher court to look at the decision. Other apps like TikTok and Snapchat settled their cases before going to court. This ruling is a big deal because it might change how social media companies operate in the future, making them more careful about how their apps affect people, especially kids.
Article_Structure: WHO: Meta and Google, the companies that own Instagram and YouTube, and Kaley, a young woman who was hurt by the apps.
WHAT: A court ruled that Instagram and YouTube are addictive and the companies must pay damages.
WHERE: Los Angeles, where the court case happened.
WHY: To hold tech companies accountable for making apps that can harm users, especially children.

perspectives.description: The court ruling shows that tech companies can be held responsible for addictive apps.
Using social media can lead to mental health problems like depression.
